title: Candle Wax on Cotton
timeEstimate: 15–40 minutes (wax itself is easy; dye residue may add a cycle)
stainChemistry: Candle wax is paraffin (a solid petroleum hydrocarbon), sometimes with dye, essential oil, or soy/beeswax additions depending on candle type. Pure wax isn't technically a stain — it's a physical deposit of solid petroleum sitting in the fiber. Dyed candles leave a separate dye stain behind AFTER the wax is removed, which is usually the actual treatment problem. Cotton tolerates freezer temperatures, gentle iron heat, POG, and 3% H₂O₂. Two-phase cleanup: remove the wax, then treat any residual dye.
whyThisWorks: Same mechanical-first logic as gum: freeze the wax brittle so it snaps off in pieces without smearing. Whatever's too thin to scrape gets absorbed by a clean paper towel with a warm iron — capillary action pulls melted wax into the absorbent paper instead of deeper into cotton. POG or IPA clears any last oily film. If the candle was dyed, residual pigment is treated like any other dye stain (peroxide + optional sunlight).
defaultAssumption: Fresh wax (on a tablecloth, shirt, etc. from a dinner candle) is a high-confidence DIY. Soy / beeswax is softer and brittle-fractures less cleanly than paraffin but the same protocol works. Dyed candles (red, black, purple) mean you're doing a two-step stain — wax first, then the color.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Zip-top bag + freezer, or ice pack
spottingProtocol.instruction: Put the waxed area in a zip-top bag and freeze 20–30 min. Or press an ice pack (in a bag) directly on the wax for the same time. Paraffin turns brittle and loses adhesion to the fiber.
Immediately post-freezer, pry the brittle wax off. It fractures and falls away cleanly. 80–95% of the wax mass leaves in this step. Work fast before it re-softens.

spottingProtocol.equipment: Brown paper (grocery bag) or clean white paper towel, iron on low-medium
spottingProtocol.instruction: Lay clean brown paper or a white paper towel over the thin wax residue. Iron on LOW-MEDIUM (no steam). The wax melts and wicks into the paper. Shift the paper to a clean section as wax appears; repeat until the paper pulls no more wax. Never iron without the absorbent paper barrier — that just drives wax deeper.

spottingProtocol.equipment: POG or IPA on a clean white cloth
spottingProtocol.instruction: Any remaining oily ring: tamp with POG or IPA on a cloth. Flip frequently. Never pour solvent directly.

spottingProtocol.technique: Tannin-style treatment of any remaining color
spottingProtocol.equipment: NSD, then 3% H₂O₂ if residual color
spottingProtocol.instruction: If the candle was dyed (red, black, purple, green), residual pigment will remain after wax removal. Apply NSD, then 3% H₂O₂ on whites. Treat the dye as its own stain class — see dye-transfer-cotton or general dye protocol.
Launder hot per care label. Inspect before the dryer.

steps: 1. Freeze to brittle. Zip-top bag 20–30 min.
3. Iron through brown paper on low-medium — wax wicks into paper by capillary action. Shift paper to clean sections.
4. Solvent tamp (POG or IPA) on residual oily ring.
5. If dyed candle: NSD + 3% H₂O₂ treats residual pigment on whites.
products: Brown paper / clean white paper towel
professionalProtocol.temperature: freezer for mechanical phase; low-medium iron for absorb; room-temp solvent; hot wash post-chemistry
warnings: Never iron without absorbent paper — drives wax deeper
Never skip the freeze — warm wax smears
Never pour solvent — tamp with cloth
Dyed candle residual color needs a separate chemistry pass

homeSolutions.instruction: Put the waxed fabric in a zip-top bag and toss it in the freezer for 20–30 minutes. Or press an ice pack (in a bag) directly on the wax. Paraffin goes hard and brittle at freezer temperatures, which is when it lifts cleanly. Skip this step and the wax smears.

Pull the fabric out and immediately pry the frozen wax off with a dull butter knife or credit-card edge. Most of it fractures and falls off in pieces. Work fast — once the wax warms back up, it re-softens and smears.

homeSolutions.agent: Iron Through a Paper Towel or Grocery Bag
homeSolutions.instruction: Place a piece of brown paper (grocery bag is perfect) or a clean white paper towel over the thin wax that's left. Iron on LOW to MEDIUM heat (no steam). The wax melts and wicks INTO the paper — you'll see it appear as a dark patch. Shift the paper to a clean section and iron again. Keep going until the paper comes away clean. NEVER iron without the absorbent paper — that drives wax deeper.

homeSolutions.agent: Goo Gone or Rubbing Alcohol for the Oily Ring
homeSolutions.instruction: An oily ring may remain. Dab Goo Gone or 70%+ rubbing alcohol on a clean white cloth and press onto the spot. Flip the cloth frequently — the oil transfers out.

homeSolutions.agent: Colored Candle? Treat the Dye Separately
homeSolutions.instruction: If the candle was dyed (red, purple, black, green), a faint pigment shadow will remain after the wax is gone. This is now a DYE stain, not a wax stain. Dab a drop of Dawn dish soap on it, work it in with a soft toothbrush for a minute, rinse. If color persists on WHITE cotton: 3% hydrogen peroxide dabbed for 3–5 min. Skip peroxide on dark or dyed cotton.

homeSolutions.agent: Hot Wash, Inspect Before the Dryer
homeSolutions.instruction: Launder on the hottest setting the care label allows. Inspect BEFORE the dryer. If there's still a ring or tint, repeat the iron-absorb OR dye-treatment step as needed.

steps: 1. Freeze 20–30 min in a zip-top bag. Or ice pack direct.
2. Scrape brittle wax off with a dull blade right after.
3. Iron through brown paper / paper towel on LOW-MED, no steam. Shift paper as wax appears.
4. Goo Gone or rubbing alcohol on a white cloth for the oily ring.
5. If colored candle: treat residual dye with Dawn + optional 3% peroxide (whites only).
6. Hot wash per care label. Inspect BEFORE the dryer.
products: Dull butter knife or credit card
Brown paper grocery bag or clean white paper towel
3% hydrogen peroxide (for dyed candle residue, whites only)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the iron-absorb step isn't lifting any more wax after several passes, OR if the dye residue from a colored candle won't fade. Take it to a dry cleaner.
warnings: NEVER iron without the absorbent paper — drives wax deeper
NEVER skip the freeze step — warm wax smears
Never pour solvent on fabric — tamp with cloth
Ventilate when using Goo Gone or alcohol

neverDo: Never iron wax directly without absorbent paper
Never try to scrub warm wax — smears into a bigger area
Never pour solvent directly on fabric
Never ignore dyed-candle pigment — treat it as a separate dye stain
fiberSensitivities: Cotton tolerates POG, IPA, 3% H₂O₂, gentle iron heat, and hot wash
Dyed cotton: skip peroxide or test inside seam first
Heat-durable fiber — iron heat is safe if the absorbent paper barrier is used

materialWarnings: FREEZE first, then IRON through paper

escalation.when: Wax has been iron-set without the absorbent paper, dye from a colored candle doesn't fade, or two full home rounds don't clear it.
escalation.whatToTell: "Candle wax on cotton — [undyed / red / black / purple candle], age [fresh / old], home-treated with freezer + iron-and-paper + Goo Gone. [Wax cleared / residue color remaining.]"

contextQuestions: Was the candle undyed (white) or dyed (red, purple, black, etc.)?
Has the fabric been ironed directly without absorbent paper?
Is the cotton white, light, or dark/dyed?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Wax is the easy half; dye from a colored candle is often the actual stain problem. Two-step treatment: freeze + iron-absorb handles the wax, then treat residual color as dye-class chemistry.
